title: Does She Thumb Her Nose at the Election Commission Too?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source-printed colloquial `பெப்பே` is a mocking expression of dismissal or contempt, used here through the village saying `உனக்கும் பெப்பே, உங்க அப்பனுக்கும் பெப்பே`. Its political force is rendered as “thumb her nose at,” rather than treating it as OCR noise. Source comparison also confirms `பழத்தின் சுளையை`, along with repairs to `முதலமைச்சருக்கு`, `உண்மைக்கு`, `தேர்தல்`, `இடைத்தேர்தலில்`, `ஜெயலலிதாவுக்கும்`, and `மனச்சாட்சிப்படி`. These repairs are confined to the derived Tamil reading copy. The printed salutation is restored; canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: Who is the Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu? What sort of person is she?

For the Yercaud Assembly by-election held on the 4th, the AIADMK appointed a “mega” election committee of sixty-one people, including thirty-one ministers, headquarters secretaries and frontline leaders. Every minister camped there. Newspapers reported the distribution of money to voters and the Chief Minister's journey by aeroplane and helicopter on the 28th to campaign at nine places in a single day. Every newspaper also reported in detail the schemes she announced for that one constituency alone.

I drew attention to Jayalalithaa's campaign announcements on the 29th itself. In my “Udanpirappu” letter published in *Murasoli* on the 30th, I explained both how the AIADMK had approached by-elections in the past and the promises the Chief Minister made in Yercaud. On my instructions that same day, party Organisation Secretary and younger brother T.K.S. Elangovan and headquarters advocate Paranthaman lodged a complaint with State Chief Electoral Officer Praveen Kumar, IAS, about Jayalalithaa's promises and the violations of the election code there.

After examining CD recordings of Jayalalithaa's speech at Kootathupatti, it was confirmed that the Chief Minister had made promises during the by-election campaign. Finding a prima facie basis for the complaint, the Election Commission issued notice directing her to reply by 5 p.m. on 3 December 2013.

In her response, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa wrote that she had made no promise or announcement in any speech on the 28th, and strongly denied the allegation that she had announced new schemes. Her explanation to the Election Commission was distributed to every newspaper and was duly published.

In the question-and-answer section under the heading “Can the Chief Minister Deceive the Election Commission?”, I responded even to that explanation. I compiled the promises she had made and how each newspaper had reported them. I also pointed out that it was a grave wrong for a person holding the office of Chief Minister to submit in writing information contrary to the truth to the Election Commission.
Yesterday, 6 December 2013, the Election Commission of India issued its order on the Chief Minister's explanation. The order said:

> The Election Commission has repeatedly stated that after an election date is announced, ministers and officials in the state where the election is being held must not promise voters any financial assistance, nor promise schemes attractive to voters such as roads and drinking-water facilities. Political parties and candidates must be provided an equal opportunity and environment in which to contest. The Supreme Court too has ruled in a case that the sanctity of elections must be protected. > > Accordingly, all political parties, candidates and prominent leaders must follow the election code wholeheartedly. The Election Commission therefore rejects the explanation you submitted in response to the notice seeking clarification for violating the rules. The Commission advises you to exercise caution in future and not issue announcements of this kind intended to attract voters.
How could the Election Commission give a Chief Minister a more telling “certificate” than this? Is this Jayalalithaa's first such certificate?

During the 2003 Sathankulam by-election, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa campaigned there and said a cement factory, women's college and polytechnic would be established. When numerous complaints were made, the Election Commission pointed to the irregularities and sought an explanation from the Tamil Nadu Chief Secretary. It then sent a letter of censure, just as it has now. The letter regretted that the Chief Secretary's explanation was unsatisfactory. It stated that the election rules prohibited the ruling party from securing an advantage by making government announcements to win an election, and that holding power did not entitle the ruling party to any concession.
The letter further said:

> We regret to state that the Election Commission does not accept the conduct of the Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu in announcing new schemes during the Sathankulam election campaign. The Commission firmly believes that the Chief Minister announced the new schemes to secure an electoral advantage. We reject Jayalalithaa's campaign method as unacceptable. The Election Commission concludes that her promises constituted a violation of the election rules.

Unfortunately, the Election Commission stopped with that letter of censure. Jayalalithaa disregarded it, and now she has once again merely been told to exercise caution in future. But are these the only occasions on which Chief Minister Jayalalithaa behaved in this way?

What did she do during the 2001 general election? Everyone knows the fundamental rule that a candidate cannot contest from more than two constituencies. Yet Jayalalithaa contested from four. At the time, she had been sentenced to three years' imprisonment in the TANSI land-deal case. Section 8 of the Representation of the People Act says that a person sentenced by a judge to imprisonment for more than two years cannot contest an election.

Though the Chief Minister knew this rule perfectly well, she deliberately contested in four constituencies. Not only that, in her nomination paper a Chief Minister gave the election officer information contrary to the truth, declaring that she was “not contesting in more than two constituencies.” Consequently, all four nominations were rejected. She deliberately created the conditions for their rejection and then campaigned to mislead the public, saying that the DMK was responsible and had prevented her from contesting, thereby gaining public sympathy.

Even while the 2001 election results were coming in, the AIADMK legislators were convened in great haste. Jayalalithaa, who had not contested and had been sentenced by a court to three years' imprisonment, had herself sworn in as Chief Minister. Newspapers such as *The Hindu* strongly condemned it. The matter later reached the courts, which ruled that her swearing-in was unconstitutional, forcing her to resign. This too was a “certificate” awarded to Chief Minister Jayalalithaa!

After the judgment, Jayalalithaa made the AIADMK member who had won Andipatti resign and decided to contest the by-election there. She then caused several price increases announced by the AIADMK government to be withdrawn. Bus fares were reduced in particular, and the increase in the price of rice was cancelled.
Lawlessness flew its flag high in the Andipatti by-election. The election officer toured the constituency and stated in his report:
> Mr. Rao returned to the Capital and in his report submitted to the Commission observed that large scale rigging and malpractices had been done in the revision of the rolls.

Irregularities and fraud occurred not only in Andipatti but in all three constituencies holding by-elections at the time. The Election Commission itself postponed two of them. This too was “fame” earned by the AIADMK government and Chief Minister Jayalalithaa!

Why, even in its judgment in the TANSI case, the Supreme Court held that Jayalalithaa, while Chief Minister, had been wrong to purchase government-owned TANSI land at a low price; that she must return the land; and that she must act according to her conscience regarding the offence she had committed. Has any Chief Minister ever received a greater “certificate” than this?
It is the explanation of such a Chief Minister that the Election Commission has now declared unacceptable, censuring her yesterday and instructing her to exercise caution in future.

Had an incident like this occurred under a DMK government, what would Jayalalithaa have done? How furiously would she have leapt up and demanded Karunanidhi's immediate resignation? I do not wish to react in anger and issue a statement as she would. But one thing comes to mind.

Today's newspapers carry a report that Jayalalithaa has removed one of her ministers from the position of district party secretary because he faces certain allegations. Will that minister not ask: “If I am punished for eating one segment of the fruit, what punishment is due to the Chief Minister who ate the whole fruit?”

Jayalalithaa imagines herself above the Constitution of India and the laws fundamental to the administration of justice in this country. She commits every kind of violation and repeatedly incurs the censure of the courts and the Election Commission of India. Will she respect her “conscience”? Will she heed “warnings”? This is the question now rising in the hearts of the people!
“I thumb my nose at you, and I thumb my nose at your father too,” says a village proverb. Under Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's rule, does she thumb her nose at the Election Commission too?
With affection,
M.K.
